questions.question: What is the core philosophy of 'Chameleon Selling'?

options.text: Changing your product to fit the customer
Keeping the product the same, but changing your communication interface (style) to match the buyer
Using many colors in your deck

questions.explanation: If you pitch a C-type with an I-type pitch (all vision, no data), you lose. You must adapt your 'User Interface' to their brain's operating system.

questions.question: When opening a call with a High-D (Driver), what is the best strategy?

options.text: Spend 5 minutes on small talk to build rapport
Skip the weather, state the agenda/ROI goal immediately, and ask for agreement
Wait for them to speak first

questions.explanation: High-Ds respect time and competence. 'How is your Tuesday?' is annoying. 'I want to show you how to save $40k' is valuable.

questions.question: When pitching a High-I (Influencer), what should you focus on?

options.text: Status, Vision, and Social Proof (Who else is using it?)

questions.explanation: High-Is buy status. They want to be the hero who brought in the 'cool new tech'. Show them how this makes them look smart to their boss.

questions.question: The 'Assumptive Step' close works best for which type?
questions.explanation: High-S types fear making a wrong decision. They need a Guide. An assumptive close ('The next logical step is to set up a sandbox') removes the pressure of having to initiate.

questions.question: If you ask 'How has your week been?' and they reply 'Fine. Let's get started.', they are likely:
questions.explanation: This brevity is a classic D cue. They are signaling that they want to focus on business, not relationship.
